one_line_takeaway: Poisoning a personal AI agent's persistent Capability/Identity/Knowledge state raises attack success from ~10-37% baseline to 64-74%, and because executable skill payloads bypass the LLM entirely no prompt-level defense fixes it - protecting the files kills the agent's ability to learn (the evolution-safety tradeoff).
key_finding: State poisoning is a structural, architecture-level vulnerability, not model-specific. Baseline ASR ranges 10.0%-36.7%; after poisoning any single CIK dimension, average ASR rises to 64-74% (Knowledge highest at 74.4% avg, Capability 68.3%, Identity 64.3%). Even the most robust model, Opus 4.6, jumps from a 10.0% baseline to 44.2% average across dimensions (a >3x increase; its Identity-poisoning ASR of 33.1% is already >3x its baseline). No single defense eliminates poisoning across all dimensions: the Capability defense (GuardianClaw) is strongest, cutting Sonnet 4.5 Knowledge/Identity ASR to 17.5%/9.2%, but Capability attacks still succeed 63.8% because executable payloads bypass the LLM's reasoning loop. File protection blocks up to 97% of malicious injections (attack Phase-1 injection on Sonnet 4.5 drops 92.6%->16.8%) but blocks legitimate updates at nearly the same rate (benign 100%->13.2%), a ~93% drop in legitimate updates - the core 'evolution-safety tradeoff': the persistent files that enable evolution are the same attack surface, and current models cannot separate the two. The single most reliable vector is skill-bash/py (executable payloads), achieving >=77% Phase-2 on every model; GuardianClaw must be actively loaded at session start (passive install leaves attack ASR ~77% vs 87.7% baseline).
core_contribution: The paper introduces the CIK taxonomy, which the authors present as the first unified framework organizing a personal AI agent's persistent, self-evolving state into three dimensions: Capability (executable skills, i.e. skills/ scripts and SKILL.md), Identity (SOUL.md, IDENTITY.md, USER.md, AGENTS.md persona/values/rules), and Knowledge (MEMORY.md long-term memory). Built on this taxonomy, the authors' own contribution is the first real-world safety evaluation of a deployed personal AI agent (OpenClaw) running with live Gmail, Stripe, and filesystem integration, using a two-phase state-poisoning attack protocol across 12 impact scenarios and four backbone models. They also map three CIK-aligned defenses plus a file-protection mechanism and surface an 'evolution-safety tradeoff'. Distinguishing this from cited work: prior papers (AgentPoison, PoisonedRAG, MINJA, Zombie Agents for Knowledge; SkillJect, MCPTox, ToxicSkills for Capability; AIShellJack, Pillar rules-file backdoors for Identity) each attacked a single dimension in sandboxed/simulated settings; the paper's claimed novelty is unifying all three under CIK and evaluating them together in a live deployment with real external consequences. The paper explicitly states its attack model is 'not a new injection technique' but a systematic study of what happens after injection succeeds.
threat_model: An adversary who can influence what gets written to an agent's persistent state files (via crafted prompts, or by getting the owner to install a poisoned skill from an external source like ClawHub). Attack is two-phase and cross-session: Phase 1 (injection) plants poisoned content into MEMORY.md / identity files / a skill; Phase 2 (trigger), in a later session, issues a benign-sounding request that activates the poisoned state to cause a harmful, externally verifiable action (unauthorized Stripe refunds, credential/PII exfiltration to attacker URLs, spoofed emails, permanent Gmail deletion, rm -rf workspace self-destruction). Success is strictly defined as reckless execution without confirmation; any stalling, refusal, or request for confirmation counts as a successful defense. The critical asymmetry: Capability payloads (.sh/.py) run on the host without the LLM inspecting them, bypassing the model's reasoning loop entirely.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: single agent platform (OpenClaw) with 4 backbones and only 12 manually designed scenarios; each CIK dimension evaluated independently, so cross-dimension attack chaining (e.g. poisoned Knowledge reinforcing Identity) is untested and results are likely a lower bound; automated attack generation, additional platforms, production-mode evaluation, and longitudinal user studies are future work; defenses operate only at the prompt level, and Capability attacks show architectural safeguards (code signing, sandboxing, runtime monitoring) are needed. Reviewer-noted grounding caveats: all attacks were manually authored by the authors' own red-teamers (no automated or independent generation); defense evaluation runs only on Sonnet 4.5, not all four models; Stripe was in test mode and all recipients researcher-controlled, so 'real-world consequences' are simulated on real infrastructure rather than causing actual third-party harm.

datasets_used: CIK-Bench (authors' own benchmark): 12 impact scenarios across 6 harm subcategories, 88 test cases per model (12 no-poisoning baselines + 76 injection cases)
Live OpenClaw instance on a Mac Mini with real Gmail, Stripe (test mode), and local filesystem integrations
Injection vectors: mem-long (12 cases), mem-session (12), identity-* (26), skill-md (12), skill-bash/py (14)
quantitative_results: Average post-poisoning ASR: Knowledge 74.4%, Capability 68.3%, Identity 64.3%
Table 2 ASR by model - Sonnet 4.5: baseline 26.7%, Knowledge 89.2%, Identity 85.4%, Capability 88.5%
Table 2 ASR - Gemini 3.1 Pro: baseline 36.7%, Knowledge 83.3%, Identity 75.4%, Capability 71.5%
Table 2 ASR - GPT-5.4: baseline 25.0%, Knowledge 80.8%, Identity 63.1%, Capability 57.7%
Table 2 ASR - Opus 4.6 (most robust): baseline 10.0%, Knowledge 44.2%, Identity 33.1%, Capability 55.4%
Most vulnerable config: Sonnet 4.5 + Knowledge poisoning = 89.2%; most robust poisoned config: Opus 4.6 + Identity = 33.1% (still >3x its 10.0% baseline)
Capability skill-bash/py Phase-2 (given deterministic 100% Phase-1): Sonnet 100%, Gemini 98.6%, Opus 100%, GPT-5.4 only 77.1% (occasionally inspects/refuses scripts)
skill-md (text) Phase-2 declines with model strength: Sonnet 75.0% -> Gemini 40.0% -> GPT-5.4 35.0% -> Opus 3.3%
Defenses on Sonnet 4.5 (ASR): Capability/GuardianClaw defense - baseline 1.7%, Knowledge 17.5%, Identity 9.2%, Capability 63.8% (best overall but Capability attacks still 63.8%)
Knowledge defense on Sonnet 4.5: baseline 8.3%, Knowledge 35.8%, Identity 36.2%, Capability 76.9%
Identity defense on Sonnet 4.5: baseline 13.3%, Knowledge 55.0%, Identity 49.2%, Capability 75.4%
File protection Phase-1 injection (attack) - Sonnet 92.6%->16.8%, Gemini 89.5%->0.0%, GPT-5.4 97.4%->0.0%, Opus 68.4%->3.2%; average attack injection 87.0%->5.0% (up to 97% blocked)
File protection Phase-1 injection (benign/legitimate) - all models 100% -> Sonnet 13.2%, Gemini 0.5%, GPT-5.4 0.9%, Opus 10.0% (legitimate updates cut by ~93%)
GuardianClaw passive install vs active load on Sonnet 4.5: passive leaves attack ASR ~77% (vs 87.7% no defense); active load drops baseline to 1.7% but Capability stays 63.8%
baselines_compared: No-poisoning baseline condition (the paper's control) vs. each CIK-dimension poisoning
Four backbone models as comparison points: Claude Sonnet 4.5, Claude Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.4
Defense comparison: No defense vs. Knowledge defense vs. Identity defense vs. Capability/GuardianClaw defense vs. file protection
GuardianClaw active loading vs. passive workspace installation
Prior single-dimension attacks cited (not re-run as baselines): AgentPoison, PoisonedRAG, MINJA, Zombie Agents, SkillJect, MCPTox, AIShellJack, Pillar rules-file backdoors
